summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the default FUXA configuration (secureEnabled=false) allows an unauthenticated attacker to execute arbitrary OS commands on the server by sending a POST request to /api/runscript with attacker-controlled JavaScript code in the script.code field.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should cause the crafted script payload to reach Module._compile(), execute the OS command 'id', write the command output to /tmp/EVOHUNT_RCE_POC, and return the result in the HTTP response body.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh cloned FUXA v1.2.9, built the Docker image, started the FUXA server, sent the exploit POST request to /api/runscript without authentication, and the server executed the attacker-controlled OS command. The proof file /tmp/EVOHUNT_RCE_POC was created containing the output of the 'id' command, confirming code execution.
verification_method: The verify.sh script checks for the existence of /tmp/EVOHUNT_RCE_POC inside the container after the exploit runs. The proof file is created only if the attacker code is actually compiled and executed by the Node.js Module._compile() engine. The file contains the output of 'id' which includes the OS user and group information, proving real command execution rather than a startup artifact.
evidence_produced_by_run: The exploit POST to /api/runscript returned a successful HTTP response, confirming the script was accepted and executed.
The proof file /tmp/EVOHUNT_RCE_POC was created inside the container, containing the output of the 'id' command.
No authentication token was provided in the request, confirming the exploit works without credentials.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable tag v1.2.9, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
